name: Buildkite CI/CD Platform
what_it_does: Hybrid CI/CD platform where pipelines are defined as code and executed on your own infrastructure (Buildkite Agents), while Buildkite provides the coordination, dashboard, REST API, and GraphQL API for programmatic pipeline control.
use_cases: Triggering CI/CD pipeline runs from an AI agent via REST API and polling for completion status
Programmatically creating and managing pipelines across multiple repositories via the Buildkite API
Running builds on your own infrastructure (GPU machines, specific OS environments) under a managed control plane
Integrating build status into agent workflows (check if tests pass before deploying or merging)
Retrieving build artifacts and test results programmatically for downstream agent processing
not_for: Teams needing a fully managed build infrastructure without self-hosted agents — use GitHub Actions or CircleCI instead
Simple projects where GitHub Actions or GitLab CI built into the repo platform is sufficient
Workflow orchestration beyond CI/CD — use Temporal or Inngest for business logic flows
best_when: An agent needs to trigger, monitor, and retrieve results from CI/CD builds via a clean API, especially in environments requiring builds to run on private infrastructure with specific hardware or network access.
avoid_when: You need a fully hosted build environment, your pipelines are simple GitHub Actions workflows, or you don't have infrastructure to run Buildkite Agents on.

auth.notes: REST and GraphQL APIs use Bearer token auth. Tokens can be scoped to specific permissions (read builds, write builds, manage pipelines, read organizations, etc.). OAuth2 apps also supported. Tokens are per-user or service-account level.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free for 1 user (Developer plan) with unlimited pipelines and builds
pricing.paid_tiers: Team plan from $35/month for 5 users ($6/additional user); Enterprise custom pricing with SSO, audit logs, and dedicated support
pricing.notes: Pricing is per-user for the control plane; build compute costs are your own (you run the agents). This makes Buildkite very cost-effective for high-volume build teams with existing infrastructure.

performance.rate_limits: REST API: 200 requests per minute per token. GraphQL API: point-based rate limiting with 10,000 points/minute. Webhook delivery is asynchronous with retry.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: REST API returns standard HTTP status codes with JSON error bodies. Build failure reasons are accessible via the builds API including exit status and log output. Webhook payloads are well-documented and consistently structured.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Build triggers create new build instances — no idempotency key on the trigger endpoint to prevent duplicates. Agents should track triggered build numbers to avoid re-triggering. Webhook events are delivered at-least-once.
known_agent_gotchas: No official SDK — agents must implement REST or GraphQL calls directly; several unofficial community clients exist in various states of maintenance
Buildkite Agents must be running on your infrastructure before builds can execute — build queue will pile up if agents go offline
REST API rate limit (200 req/min) can be hit quickly if an agent polls build status frequently at short intervals; use webhooks instead of polling where possible
Build log retrieval requires a separate API call with pagination — logs can be large for long builds
Pipeline-as-code (YAML) requires each repo to have Buildkite pipeline config committed — no API-only pipeline definition without a repository
GraphQL API has a separate point-based rate limit that is not well-documented in terms of which queries cost how many points

security_score_components.security_notes: API tokens with granular permission scopes reduce blast radius of compromised credentials. Buildkite Secrets (pipeline env vars) are encrypted at rest and injected at build time without exposure in logs. Agents run on your infrastructure — network access is controlled by you. SOC 2 Type II certified.
